message.content:
Feature: MHPCO Claim Office (quote + claim operations)
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: See prompt.md in the run root for the full specification. The system supports two operations: `quote` (computes premium for a list of items) and `claim` (processes a damage report against an existing policy).

Key rules summary:
- Item base premiums: sword 100G/1000G value, amulet 60G/600G, staff 80G/800G, potion 40G/400G
- Components (rune, moonstone): 25G premium / 250G value each; block of exactly 3 alike components = 60G base premium
- Premium modifiers: cursed +50% (item-specific), high enchantment (level>=5) +30% (item-specific), loyalty -20% (>=2 years, policy-wide), first insurance +10% (per item), follow-up contract -15% (policy-wide, contracts after first), +5G processing fee at end
- Item-specific modifiers apply to that item's base premium; policy-wide modifiers apply to policy base premium sum; fee added last
- Rounding: premium rounds UP, payout rounds DOWN (always in MHPCO favor); only final amount rounded
- Claim: 100G deductible per damage event (per item), payout capped at 2x insurance sum; enchantment>=8 reimbursed at 50%; dragon material fully reimbursed; cap persists across successive claims
- Error cases: unknown item type, claim referencing item not in policy, negative damage amount, more damage entries of a type than insured -> CLI non-zero exit + stderr
- CLI at src/cli.ts reads JSON scenario from stdin, writes {results:[...]} to stdout

Read prompt.md fully for all examples and edge cases.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, no HITL checkpoints.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the full specification in prompt.md.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

content.text:
The src directory is empty. Now I'll create the example mapping file and the test list. Let me first work through the calculations carefully to verify expected values.

Let me reason through the key examples to build the example mapping:

**Block premiums (runes at 25G each):** 2 runes=50, 3 runes=60 (block), 4 runes=100, 7 runes=175 (one block of 3 = 60, plus 4 singles at 25 = 100, total 160... wait). Let me recompute: 7 runes → spec says 175. So 7 = block(3)=60 + 4 singles=100 = 160? That's 160 not 175. Let me reconsider: maybe one block of 3=60, remaining 4 singles=100 → 160. But spec says 175. Alternatively two blocks? No, 7 isn't divisible. Let me reconsider — maybe blocks are not auto-applied for 7. 7 singles = 175G. The spec lists "7 runes → 175 G" which equals 7×25=175. So blocks only apply when the count is exactly a multiple giving best... Actually 3 runes→60 (block applies automatically), 4 runes→100 (no block, requires exactly 3). For 7: 7×25=175. So the rule: a block applies only when the group has EXACTLY 3 of an alike type. With 7 runes, it's not exactly 3, so no block — all singles → 175. Good, consistent with "block requires exactly 3."

Let me verify the "alike" examples: 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 (2×25 + 1×25 = 75, no block since not 3 of same type). 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 (block of 3 runes=60 + block of 3 moonstones=60 = 120). Good — "alike" means exactly same type.

Now let me work through the integration and claim examples to confirm values.

**Newcomer cursed sword:** base 100 + curse 50 + first-insurance 10 = 160, + fee 5 = 165. ✓

**Long-standing 2nd contract cursed sword enchant 7:** base 100 + curse 50 (item) + high-ench 30 (item) = 180 item subtotal. Policy base = 100. Policy-wide on policy base 100: loyalty -20 (20% of 100), first-insurance +10 (10% per item = 10), follow-up -15 (15% of 100). So 180 + (-20 + 10 - 15) = 180 - 25 = 155, + fee 5 = 160. ✓

**Cursed sword premium with modifiers 165** (cap example, newcomer): same as 165 above but spec says "premium with modifiers 165G" → matches newcomer. ✓

**Multi-item: cursed sword + plain amulet:** base 100+60=160, curse +50 → 210 before further modifiers and fee. ✓

**Claim examples** confirmed in summary. Now writing the example mapping file.

input.content:
# Example Mapping: MHPCO Claim Office

Feature for `src/claim-office.ts` (logic) + `src/cli.ts` (CLI). Tests in
`src/claim-office.spec.ts`.

Two operations: **quote** (premium for a list of items) and **claim**
(payout for a damage report against an existing policy).

---

## Rules

### R1 — Item base premiums & insurance values
| type      | insurance value | base premium |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| sword     | 1000 G          | 100 G        |
| amulet    | 600 G           | 60 G         |
| staff     | 800 G           | 80 G         |
| potion    | 400 G           | 40 G         |
| component (rune, moonstone) | 250 G each | 25 G each |

### R2 — Component block
A group of **exactly 3 alike** components (same type) has a special base
premium of 60 G (instead of 3×25=75). "Alike" = exactly the same type
(rune ≠ moonstone). Block applies only at exactly 3; 4+ alike are priced
as singles. Insurance value/sum is unaffected by the block (still 250 G
each).

### R3 — Premium modifiers
- **Cursed**: +50% surcharge — item-specific (on that item's base premium).
- **High enchantment** (level ≥ 5): +30% surcharge — item-specific.
- **Loyalty** (≥ 2 years with MHPCO): −20% discount — policy-wide.
- **First insurance**: +10% surcharge — applied per item; each item in a
  quote is always treated as a first insurance regardless of customer
  history.
- **Follow-up contract**: −15% discount — policy-wide; applies to every
  quote after the customer's first quote in the scenario.
- **Processing fee**: +5 G flat, added at the very end.

Item-specific modifiers apply to the affected item's base premium.
Policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, follow-up) apply to the policy base
premium = sum of all item base premiums. First insurance is +10% per
item (10% of each item's base premium). Fee added last.

### R4 — Rounding
Only the final amount is rounded. Premium rounds **UP**; payout rounds
**DOWN** (always in MHPCO's favor). Intermediate values kept as fractions.

### R5 — Claim processing
- 100 G deductible per damage event (per damaged item entry).
- Payout per policy capped at **2× insurance sum** (based on unmodified
  insurance values; premium modifiers do not raise the cap).
- Enchantment ≥ 8: damage reimbursed at 50% of damage amount, then deductible.
- Dragon material: fully reimbursed, then deductible.
- If both apply (dragon + ench ≥ 8): the 50% rule wins.
- Components (rune/moonstone) have no enchantment/material → no special clause.
- Cap persists across successive claims on the same policy.

### R6 — Error cases (CLI: non-zero exit + stderr, no results on stdout)
- Unknown item type in a quote (e.g. "broomstick").
- Claim references a damage entry whose item is not in the policy
  (wrong type, or unknown type).
- Damage entry with negative amount (e.g. -200).
- More damage entries of a given type than the policy insures.

### R7 — CLI / scenario
- `claim-office` CLI at `src/cli.ts` reads JSON scenario from stdin,
  writes `{results: [...]}` to stdout.
- Scenario: `{customer:{yearsWithMHPCO}, steps:[...]}`.
- Steps processed sequentially; a `claim` references the quote step that
  created its policy via zero-based `policy` index.
- `results` array same length/order as `steps`.

---

## Examples (with expected values)

### Block (R2)
- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block; needs exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium (7×25; not exactly 3, no block)
- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G (no block, different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G (two separate blocks: 60 + 60)

### Modifier scope (R3)
- cursed sword (base 100) + plain amulet (base 60) → policy base 160 G;
  curse adds 50 G (50% of sword base) → 210 G before further modifiers & fee.

### Modifier thresholds (R3, R5)
- exactly 2 years → loyalty applies.
- sword enchantment exactly 5 → high-enchantment surcharge applies.
- sword enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge.
- dragon sword, enchantment exactly 8, damage 1000 → payout 400 G
  (50% rule wins: 500 − 100 deductible).

### Deductible per event (R5)
- dragon attack damages sword (500) + amulet (300) → payout 600 G
  (400 + 200, deductible once per damaged item).

### Standard reimbursement (R5)
- steel sword, ench 3, damage 500 → payout 400 G.
- rune (value 250), damage 200 → payout 100 G (200 − 100; no clause).

### Enchantment threshold vs dragon material (R5)
- dragon sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (50% wins: 500 − 100).
- dragon sword, ench 5, damage 800 → payout 700 G (dragon only: 800 − 100).
- steel sword, ench 9, damage 1000 → payout 400 G (high-ench: 500 − 100).

### Multiple items of same type (R1, R5, R6)
- two swords → insurance sum 2000 G, cap 4000 G.
- dragon attack with two sword damage entries → each its own deductible.
- more sword damage entries than swords insured → CLI non-zero exit, claim rejected.

### Cap (R5)
- sword + amulet → insurance sum 1600 G, cap 3200 G.
- cursed sword (premium with modifiers 165 G) → cap 2000 G (unmodified value).
- sword + 3 runes (block) → insurance sum 1750 G (1000 + 3×250); block
  affects premium only.
- sword (sum 1000, cap 2000), two successive claims of 1500 each:
  - first claim → payout 1400 G (1500 − 100), cap remaining 600 G.
  - second claim → payout 600 G (desired 1400 capped to remaining), cap remaining 0 G.

### Rounding (R4)
- premium 197.5 G → 198 G (up).
- payout 350.5 G → 350 G (down).

### Edge cases (R1, R6)
- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the fee).
- quote with unknown type (broomstick) → CLI non-zero exit + stderr, no results.
- claim references item not in policy (amulet damaged, only sword insured) →
  CLI non-zero exit + stderr.
- claim damage amount -200 → CLI non-zero exit + stderr.

### Integration examples
- **Newcomer cursed sword** (0 years, no prior contract; steel, ench 3):
  premium 165 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 10 first-insurance = 160 + 5 fee.
- **Long-standing 2nd contract** (3 years, 2nd quote; cursed sword steel ench 7):
  premium 160 G = 100 base + 50 curse + 30 high-ench − 20 loyalty
  + 10 first-insurance − 15 follow-up = 155 + 5 fee.

---

## Interface contract

**Input (stdin), verbatim field names:**
```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {"op": "quote", "items": [
      {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
    ]},
    {"op": "claim", "policy": 0, "incident": {
      "cause": "fire",
      "damages": [{"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}]
    }}
  ]
}
```

**Output (stdout):**
```json
{"results": [{"premium": 71}, {"payout": 100, "remainingCap": 1100}]}
```
- quote result: `{premium: <integer>}`.
- claim result: `{payout: <integer>, remainingCap: <integer>}`.

Item types: `sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone`. Item
fields `material`, `enchantment`, `cursed` are optional. Components have
no enchantment/material.

The core logic in `claim-office.ts` may expose functions (e.g. `quote`,
`claim`, or a single `runScenario`); `cli.ts` wires stdin/stdout. Exact
internal function names are free, but the JSON field names above are binding.

---

## Questions / Clarifications (❓ resolved)

- ❓ "Alike" components → **exactly the same type** (rune ≠ moonstone).
- ❓ Modifier scope on multi-item policies → item-specific modifiers
  (cursed, high-ench) on the item's base; policy-wide (loyalty,
  first-insurance per item, follow-up) on policy base; fee last.
- ❓ Two of the same item → each is a separate insured item; each damage
  entry is a separate event with its own deductible; too many damage
  entries of a type → reject claim (non-zero exit).
- ❓ "First insurance" → every item in a quote is always treated as a
  first insurance regardless of customer history; follow-up discount is
  separate and policy-wide.

---

## Per-test rationale (maps to it.todo order)

1. empty item list → 5 G (fee only) — edge case, simplest quote.
2. single sword newcomer-neutral base path — R1 base premium + fee.
3. amulet / staff / potion base premiums — R1 each main item.
4. single rune → 25+fee — R1 component premium.
5. 2 runes → 50 base — R2 below-block.
6. 3 runes → 60 base (block) — R2 block applies.
7. 4 runes → 100 base (no block) — R2 exactly-3 boundary.
8. 7 runes → 175 base — R2 not-exactly-3, all singles.
9. 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 — R2 different types, no block.
10. 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 — R2 two separate blocks.
11. cursed sword surcharge (item-specific) — R3 cursed.
12. high-enchantment ench 5 boundary applies — R3 high-ench threshold.
13. enchantment 4 → no high-ench — R3 threshold negative.
14. cursed + high-ench stack on one item — R3 both item-specific.
15. loyalty ≥2 years (exactly 2) — R3 loyalty policy-wide.
16. first-insurance +10% per item — R3 first insurance.
17. follow-up −15% on 2nd quote — R3 follow-up policy-wide.
18. multi-item modifier scope: cursed sword + amulet → 210 before fee — R3 scope.
19. newcomer cursed sword integration → 165 — R3 integration.
20. long-standing 2nd contract integration → 160 — R3 integration.
21. premium rounds up 197.5 → 198 — R4.
22. claim: steel sword ench3 damage 500 → 400 — R5 standard.
23. claim: rune damage 200 → 100 — R5 component no clause.
24. claim: deductible per event, sword 500 + amulet 300 → 600 — R5.
25. claim: dragon sword ench8 damage 1000 → 400 — R5 50% wins at 8.
26. claim: dragon sword ench9 damage 1000 → 400 — R5 both, 50% wins.
27. claim: dragon sword ench5 damage 800 → 700 — R5 dragon only.
28. claim: steel sword ench9 damage 1000 → 400 — R5 high-ench only.
29. payout rounds down 350.5 → 350 — R4.
30. cap = 2× insurance sum (sword+amulet 1600 → cap 3200) — R5 cap value.
31. cap based on unmodified value (cursed sword → cap 2000) — R5 cap.
32. insurance sum with block (sword + 3 runes → 1750) — R5/R2 sum vs premium.
33. two swords → insurance sum 2000, cap 4000 — R5/R1.
34. successive claims first 1400 cap-remaining 600 — R5 cap persists.
35. successive claims second 600 cap-remaining 0 — R5 cap exhaustion.
36. CLI: reads stdin scenario, writes results to stdout — R7 happy path.
37. CLI: quote then claim sequential, claim references policy index — R7.
38. CLI error: unknown item type (broomstick) → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
39. CLI error: claim item not in policy → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
40. CLI error: negative damage amount → non-zero exit + stderr — R6.
41. CLI error: more damage entries of a type than insured → non-zero exit — R6.

vitest binary isn't installed yet, but the test list is structurally valid and uses only `it.todo()`, so it requires no implementation. The dependency installation is part of later phases. The test list and example mapping are complete.

📋 Test List Created:

**Feature**: MHPCO Claim Office (quote + claim operations)
**Test File**: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts
**Example Mapping**: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_12-26-27_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/example-mapping/claim-office.md
**Tests**: 41 `it.todo()` cases, ordered simple → complex

**Test Cases** (grouped, ordered simple → complex):
1-6. Base premiums: empty list (5G fee only), sword, amulet, staff, potion, single rune
7-12. Component block (R2): 2/3/4/7 runes, rune+moonstone mixes, two blocks
13-16. Item-specific modifiers (R3): cursed, high-ench at 5, ench 4 negative, both stacked
17-19. Policy-wide modifiers (R3): loyalty (exactly 2yr), first-insurance, follow-up
20. Multi-item modifier scope: cursed sword + amulet → 210 before fee
21-22. Integration: newcomer cursed sword → 165; long-standing 2nd contract → 160
23. Premium rounds up (197.5 → 198)
24-25. Standard claims: steel sword → 400; rune → 100
26. Deductible per event: sword + amulet → 600
27-30. Enchantment vs dragon material: ench8→400, ench9 dragon→400, ench5 dragon→700, steel ench9→400
31. Payout rounds down (350.5 → 350)
32-37. Cap rules: sum/cap values, unmodified-value cap, block vs sum, two swords, two successive claims (1400/600 then 600/0)
38-39. CLI happy path: stdin→stdout results; sequential quote+claim by policy index
40-43. CLI errors: unknown type, item-not-in-policy, negative amount, too-many-damage-entries → non-zero exit + stderr

Every spec rule, example, and ❓ clarification maps to at least one test (mapping documented in the per-test rationale). All four CLI error conditions and the CLI stdin/stdout surface are covered as explicit tests, not deferred. The four ❓ ambiguities are resolved in the example mapping and each has a corresponding test (alike→types, modifier scope→test 20, multiple-same-item→tests 33+43, first-insurance→test 21/22).

Note: the `vitest` binary is not yet installed in node_modules (`pnpm test` reports `vitest: not found`); dependency install/running happens in the Red phase. The test list itself is valid and uses only `it.todo()`.
